author: @Vidyut_Gore
text: I've been told I have it too. Don't like being interrupted when engrossed, creature of habit, asocial, tend to dig into projects... I don't see the problem. It is a feature, not bug. Superpower rather than disorder. Personally, I just call it being task focused. I think we confuse people, particularly those who tend to get distracted and not complete things. Isn't us with the problem, is it.

author: @townbell2248
text: These labels make some companies money.  I know many parents who are giving their children medicine for it. The medicine used seems questionable to me but that is my opinion and I will pass on having my child take that stuff

author: @ryanjames1501
text: Seems like a lot of us who watch your channel have the same 'issue' (or blessing rather) and I reckon a lot of us technical folk are like that :)

author: @rowanshole
text:
ADHD is two different thing 1)Attention deficit
2) hyper activity.

Children, especially boy children are by their very nature hyper. If they are not hyper they become lethargic blobs.

In times past children got plenty of attention, or work and things were pretty much okay, but now days with both parents in the workforce, mega mortgages, large class sizes and suspicion against children roaming we have a problem.

These children require too much attention- attention people are too busy to give.

Thus the attention deficit doesent reside with the children  but is something they have to endure as lacking from busy parents, sovial systems and Institutions such as schools. 

And hyperactivity? Well who has ever heard of that state in children? 

DRUGS!

That will fix it right!

author: @steini19o4
text:
There are some other factors to it, too, tho.

Like very rarely having a refreshing sleep, no matter how long, being on time or following a schedule being an almost impossible task, severe difficulty remembering important short term tasks, like someone asks you to do something in ten minutes and you just don't remember it pretty much ever, frequently forgetting important items, like your keys, money or umbrella, and constantly making mistakes during routine tasks or missing important points in talks when they come up without noticing it.

Some people with severe ADHD are pretty much walking zombies, constantly being in a state of tiredness normal people wouldn't experience even after several days without sleep, but they don't notice it, since all cases of ADHD start in early childhood, and they think this is just normal and everyone else is coping with the same stage of suffering just way better than them. Medication like Medikinet adult or Ritalin adult (retarded for longer effect) can be incredibly life changing.

author: @martinfields373
text: Some people manifest this more profoundly than others.  I had to tell my son years ago when he was in high school, "We're not like other people."  The sooner you accept that, the easier it is to get on with your life.

author: @JustavvaGo
text:
Welcome to the club Robert.  I watched ''The Good Doctor'' series and started, myself,  to think about if i might have this ''Label''.
Yes, i read a lot about it. The more i read about it the more i'm convinced. Anyway, use it to your advantage and don't forget, you're not alone and it's more of a godsend than a curse.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
The main guy has autism.
Kinda opposite of ADHD

author: @swedishdad
text: It can be both a blessing and a curse. The curse part manifests if/when coping or success comes at the expense of the well-being of our loved ones and our relationships (ie. if someone else has to compensate for our more dysfunctional habits, to their detriment). Without that element, it may as well be numbered among a host of other idiosyncrasies that just serve to add color to our lives :)

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
I was diagnosed ADHD and Autistic last year ( 59 at the time ) which wasn't much of a surprise to people who've known me for long. It was almost amusing having the Dr trying to deal with me gently, leading up to telling me about the ADHD ( I'd already been told about the autism ) , with  "..well it's a big thing, how will you feel if you find out you are?" etc
I stopped him with 
"No it's not a big deal, I am what I am, it just means you've got a label for it now"  

I probably kept under the radar for so long as the 2 conditions have some very opposite symptoms. Also if my mind's racing with a thousand thoughts a minutes I'll use that for thinking about creative things, if it's in hyper-focus mode I'll use that for academic/logic/learning things. If I may paraphrase something you've often said about tools...

"It's about using the right 'disability' for the right job"  ;-)

author: @augustfassler9180
text:
Welcome to the club, brother! I was 51 last year and finally started to realize it myself. When I told a good friend, his response verbatim was "well, duh!"

Apparently those close to me knew it all along and I was blissfully unaware that people didn't think the same or even similarly to how I think. Since then I've done a lot of research and started realizing how much of the way I work and live is via a series of complex coping mechanisms because the majority of the world around me thinks differently.

I'm short, I've come to realize that in some areas it can be a challenge or deficiency, but in others it's certainly a superpower! Start looking into ADHD superpowers and I think you'll recognize a few of them.

At the start of this comment, I said "welcome to the club". That didn't mean the ADHD club - I've seen you as a member for some time. I meant the "I'm a grown ass adult and just figured out I'm ADHD" club.

author: @TobaJones77
text:
You'll never find a boring person with ADHD.

I was diagnosed at 37. Came as a shock to me! 

ADHD brings incredible curiosity and creativity. The world is better for it.

author: @coltenwatkins6058
text: Not a problem, just divergent from the norm.

author: @robhudson1501
text: These things can be hereditary, I have dyspraxia which shares many commonalities but also puts me at a distinct disadvantage to yourself. For instance I am in awe of people who can walk and talk at the same time without tripping up or falling over. But I like to make things and I do my best. Of course I often have to settle for less than brilliant or good but I still get the satisfaction of doing it.

author: @sfco1299
text:
I'm right there with you Rob. My wife said the same thing to me about 6 months ago. It's been quite interesting to explore things I've always considered to be my 'quirks' from this new perspective. Wholeheartedly agree that it can be a massive advantage. Through work I'm often pointed at a problem and 'let go' to burrow into a hyperfocus and solve it. This is absolutely a blessing in my current line of work, but I had a very turbulent time before I discovered a career in something that interested me enough to let my focus be on my work, rather than extracurricular activities.
An interesting note - ADHD is 'treated' with stimulants, which is why a lot of us who don't realise we probably have it until well into our adult lives wind up drinking plenty of strong coffee all day - We are self-medicating!
